doc_type: Administrative Complaint — California Board of Behavioral Sciences (BBS) against Licensed Marriage and Family Therapist
key_facts: Licensee: Katherine A. Palacios, LMFT, License No. 44555, License Status: Renewed & Current (Exp. February 28, 2027), Title: Clinical Director, HOPE Program (HOPE Psychotherapy, Inc.), 5403 N. Pershing Avenue, Suite 200, Stockton, CA 95207

HOPE is a CASOMB-certified  treatment provider contracting with CDCR to deliver mandatory treatment under California Penal Code § 3008(d); Palacios exercises delegated governmental authority over liberty-affecting treatment determinations
Complainant: Brendan Ngehsi Newanforbi, , Stockton, CA 95204; complaint filed February 21, 2026

At an August 2025 Case Conference/Containment Team Meeting (CTM) at a CDCR parole office in Stockton, Palacios publicly stated to all attendees — including parole agents and supervisors — that Newanforbi was 'disruptive,' a 'ringleader,' that his 'behavior is abhorrent,' that he was 'often disrespectful' to HOPE staff, and characterized his respectful conduct at the meeting as 'quite frankly impressive' acting

Palacios herself acknowledged Newanforbi's respectful conduct at the CTM meeting before making those statements, demonstrating actual knowledge that her subsequent characterizations contradicted her own contemporaneous observations

The 'ringleader' label was explicitly predicated on Newanforbi voicing complaints — 'because when you start complaining then the other guys chime in' — i.e., the label was tied directly to the exercise of protected expression

Agent Chow (CDCR), who had extensive firsthand experience with Newanforbi, reacted with visible surprise immediately after Palacios's remarks, stating 'Newanforbi, what's going on?' — corroborating the falsity of her characterizations

Following the CTM meeting, Newanforbi curtailed his speech during HOPE group sessions out of fear of retaliation; Palacios subsequently remarked he was 'doing better' and claimed she was 'putting in a good word' for him with his parole agent — demonstrating she equated his silence with improved conduct, confirming retaliatory motive

Palacios's CTM statements were incorporated into CDCR Form 1502-DR Discharge Review Report (dated November 12, 2025), which relied on input from both Palacios and Agent Derrick and recommended Newanforbi's continued retention on parole

HOPE operates a blanket retention policy that categorically refuses to certify 'completion' for any parolee prior to parole discharge, regardless of clinical progress, compliance, risk reduction, or time in treatment — a policy documented under oath by HOPE's own Executive Director of Clinical Training in In re Frederick Dwyer, No. SC-080355A (San Mateo Superior Court, March 20, 2023), where the court found it 'vitiates the Legislature's directive' under Penal Code § 3008(d)(2)

Despite judicial condemnation in Dwyer and the April 17, 2025 enactment of Title 15 CCR § 3574(f)(1) — which explicitly states 'Treatment completion and parole discharge are legally separate decisions' — Palacios continued applying HOPE's blanket retention policy through November 2025 and beyond

Palacios represented to CDCR that Newanforbi's 'non-completion' status was the product of individualized clinical assessment when she knew it was the automatic output of a blanket policy applied uniformly to every participant

HOPE's treatment structure requires participants to complete the 'Core' phase twice, the 'Maintenance' phase twice, and then a 'titration' phase — a framework appearing nowhere in Penal Code § 3008(d) or Title 15 CCR § 3574 — resulting in participants remaining in treatment for 5–7+ years with no meaningful prospect of completion

Palacios never provided Newanforbi with: written criteria for program completion; written explanation of phase advancement requirements; an appeal process for titration or phase-advancement decisions; or any mechanism to challenge lost homework or forced phase restarts

Palacios admitted homework assignments are 'routinely lost' within HOPE, yet participants are forced to restart treatment phases due to staff turnover and administrative failures, forfeiting previously earned progress

HOPE operates a fee-for-service billing model; per Alameda County Probation Dept. Contract No. 26374 (First Amendment, January 9, 2024): $100/group session, $100/CTM meeting per client, $200/individual session, $500/polygraph, $1,800/psychosexual evaluation, $125/quarterly treatment plan

A single parolee attending two group sessions per week generates approximately $10,600 annually; over Newanforbi's 10-year parole term this represents approximately $106,000 in baseline revenue; HOPE's contracts provide no compensation incentive for successful treatment completion — revenue terminates upon discharge

Palacios, as Clinical Director, controls the 'completion' determination that CDCR uses as a gatekeeper for discharge eligibility while possessing a direct, ongoing, undisclosed financial interest in ensuring that determination is never favorable

Parole supervision was immediately escalated from once to twice per month following Palacios's CTM statements; Agent Derrick recommended the increase and the supervising parole agent approved it on the spot

Newanforbi documented lost wages of no less than $109,408 for 2022–2024: but-for CDL earning capacity of $72,000/year against actual W-2 earnings of $39,553 (2022), $11,145 (2023), and $55,894 (2024); losses continue accruing at approximately $6,000/month

Newanforbi experienced physical symptoms of chilling: increased heart rate, shaking hands, and nervousness during parole interactions following the CTM meeting; he abandoned written complaints as a direct result

dates.event: Alameda County Probation Dept. Procurement Contract No. 26374 — First Amendment establishing HOPE billing rates approved

In re Frederick Dwyer, No. SC-080355A (San Mateo Superior Court) — court order condemning HOPE's blanket retention policy as vitiat[ing] the Legislature's directive under Penal Code § 3008(d)(2); based on sworn testimony by HOPE's own Executive Director of Clinical Training
Title 15 CCR § 3574(f)(1) effective — explicitly provides that 'Treatment completion and parole discharge are legally separate decisions'

Case Conference/Containment Team Meeting (CTM) at CDCR parole office, Stockton — Palacios made defamatory and retaliatory statements; parole supervision escalated from monthly to twice-monthly on the spot
CDCR Form 1502-DR Discharge Review Report prepared, incorporating Palacios's 'disruptive' and 'ringleader' characterizations; recommended continued retention on parole
BBS Complaint filed by Brendan Ngehsi Newanforbi against Katherine A. Palacios, LMFT 44555

arguments: Palacios violated her duty to provide individualized clinical assessments by applying HOPE's blanket retention policy — which forecloses completion for every participant prior to parole discharge — rather than assessing Newanforbi's actual clinical progress, compliance, or risk reduction

Palacios violated her duty to maintain professional objectivity free from financial conflicts by controlling the completion determination while operating under a billing model that financially rewards indefinite retention and penalizes discharge; this conflict was undisclosed

Palacios violated her duty not to misrepresent clinical determinations by representing to CDCR that Newanforbi's 'non-completion' status was the product of individualized clinical judgment when she knew it was the automatic output of a blanket policy applied uniformly to every participant

Palacios violated her duty not to retaliate against clients for protected expression: her August 2025 CTM statements were made in direct response to Newanforbi's constitutionally protected complaints about program conditions; she confirmed the retaliatory nature of her conduct by equating his subsequent silence with improvement and claiming to advocate for him with his parole agent

Palacios violated her duty to provide transparent treatment criteria by operating a program with unpublished, internally contradictory phase-progression and completion standards; she failed to provide written criteria, a meaningful completion pathway, or any appeal or challenge mechanism

Palacios violated her duty to maintain accurate records by admitting homework is 'routinely lost' while failing to implement safeguards, and by forcing participants to restart treatment phases due to her program's own administrative failures

Palacios violated her duty to comply with applicable law and regulation by continuing to apply HOPE's blanket retention policy after: (a) the March 2023 Dwyer order specifically condemned it; and (b) the April 2025 Title 15 CCR § 3574(f)(1) amendments enacted to remedy those deficiencies

The 'ringleader' label constituted a false statement of fact — Palacios's own predicate ('because when you start complaining then the other guys chime in') confirms the label tracked protected speech, not orchestrated insubordination; it was published to governmental officials controlling Newanforbi's liberty

The 'acting job' characterization communicated to all officials present that Newanforbi was fundamentally deceptive and that his good behavior could not be trusted — a false statement about character designed to permanently undermine his credibility with the officials who controlled his liberty

Palacios's conduct constitutes an abuse of professional authority: she wielded the therapist-clinical-director role not to serve the client's clinical interests but to punish protected expression and suppress advocacy through false characterizations delivered to liberty-controlling government officials

The blanket retention policy, combined with mandatory phase repetitions not grounded in statute or regulation and systematic loss of completed homework, rendered Newanforbi's 'non-complete' status predetermined from the day he entered the program — a misrepresentation of clinical reality to CDCR

The financial conflict of interest is structural: revenue terminates upon discharge; HOPE has no documented completion incentive; Palacios holds sole authority over the completion determination; this creates an irreconcilable conflict between her financial interest and her professional duty to the client
evidence_cited: Verbatim statements made by Palacios at August 2025 CTM: 'Mr. Newanforbi, I am impressed with your performance today. You present a completely opposite side at the HOPE group, wouldn't you agree?' and 'You're disruptive during HOPE group sessions, often deviating from the curriculum to talk how you disagree with the treatment. You're a ringleader, because when you start complaining then the other guys chime in. Your behavior is abhorrent and you're often disrespectful to my staff. Quite frankly I'm shocked at the acting job you've put on today, it is quite frankly impressive.'

Agent Chow's spontaneous reaction immediately after Palacios's statements: 'Newanforbi, what's going on?' — corroborating the disconnect between Palacios's characterization and Chow's firsthand knowledge of Newanforbi

Palacios's post-CTM statements during HOPE group sessions that Newanforbi was 'doing better' and that she had been 'putting in a good word' for him with his parole agent — after he curtailed his speech

CDCR Form 1502-DR Discharge Review Report dated November 12, 2025 — incorporates Palacios and Agent Derrick inputs; recommends 'Retain on Parole'; offered as evidence that false CTM characterizations were laundered into official governmental records affecting liberty

In re Frederick Dwyer, No. SC-080355A (San Mateo Superior Court, March 20, 2023) — order and sworn testimony of HOPE's own Executive Director of Clinical Training documenting the blanket retention policy and judicial finding that it 'vitiates the Legislature's directive' under Penal Code § 3008(d)(2)

Alameda County Probation Department Procurement Contract No. 26374 (First Amendment, January 9, 2024) — establishes HOPE billing rates: $100/group session, $100/CTM per client, $200/individual session, $500/polygraph, $1,800/psychosexual evaluation, $125/quarterly treatment plan
Title 15 CCR § 3574(f)(1) (effective April 17, 2025) — 'Treatment completion and parole discharge are legally separate decisions'
California Penal Code § 3008(d) — statutory framework for mandatory  treatment and completion certification

W-2 wage statements for 2022–2024: $39,553 (2022), $11,145 (2023), $55,894 (2024) — documenting supervision-suppressed CDL earnings against but-for earning capacity of $72,000/year; total documented loss $109,408
BBS License Verification confirming active LMFT License No. 44555 for Katherine A. Palacios

Filed Complaint for Damages in San Joaquin County Superior Court, Case No. STK-CV-UF-2026-1032 (Newanforbi v. HOPE Psychotherapy, Inc., et al.) — asserting fraud, negligence, false imprisonment, IIED, and interference with prospective economic advantage
Filed Complaint for Damages (Defamation — Slander Per Se and Libel Per Se) against Katie A. Palacios in San Joaquin County Superior Court — arising from the August 2025 CTM statements
First Amended Complaint and service documentation in Newanforbi v. Derrick, et al., Case No. 2:26-cv-00004-DJC-JDP (E.D. Cal.) — federal civil rights claims arising from the same course of conduct
Palacios's admission that homework assignments are 'routinely lost' within HOPE Program — while participants are forced to restart phases due to staff turnover and administrative failures
procedural_significance: This BBS complaint runs in parallel with and mutually reinforces the civil claims in Newanforbi v. Palacios et al. (STK-CV-UF-2026-0001032, San Joaquin Superior Court) in several critical respects. First, the complaint's factual record — verbatim defamatory statements, Palacios's own admission of the retaliatory predicate ('because when you start complaining'), Agent Chow's corroborating reaction, the post-CTM 'doing better'/'putting in a good word' admissions, and the CDCR Form 1502-DR incorporating the false characterizations — provides the core evidentiary backbone for the civil fraud, negligence, IIED, and slander per se claims. Second, the blanket retention policy, documented here as a continuing regulatory violation of Title 15 CCR § 3574(f)(1) and judicially condemned in In re Dwyer (March 2023), directly supports the civil fraud and negligence theories: Palacios misrepresented a policy-driven output as an individualized clinical judgment to a governmental agency making liberty-affecting decisions. Third, the financial conflict of interest documented through the Alameda County contract billing rates and the structural absence of any completion incentive establishes the motive element common to both the BBS ethics violations and the civil fraud and IIED claims. Fourth, the chilling-effect sequence — escalated parole supervision immediately after CTM, speech self-censorship, Palacios rewarding silence — supplies direct evidence of actual malice and retaliatory intent for both the defamation claims and any First Amendment-grounded civil rights theories. Fifth, a BBS finding of professional misconduct or license discipline would constitute a quasi-judicial admission of the very violations underlying the civil tort claims (duty breach, misrepresentation, retaliation), and BBS investigation records and any adverse findings would be admissible in the civil proceedings. The complaint was filed February 21, 2026 — after the civil action was filed — and explicitly cross-references the San Joaquin Superior Court case and the federal civil rights action (Newanforbi v. Derrick, 2:26-cv-00004-DJC-JDP, E.D. Cal.), creating a coordinated multi-forum record of misconduct. The 'grace-period revocation' dynamic is captured in the post-CTM sequence: Palacios used her clinical authority to escalate supervision, waited for Newanforbi to go silent, and then conditionally withdrew adverse characterizations — conduct that in the civil context supports punitive damages theories and in the BBS context demonstrates a sustained, willful abuse of professional authority rather than an isolated lapse.
